coredata.dc:description: Antibiotic residues in water are closely related to human health and environment. Therefore, it is of great significance to detect and remove antibiotics in water. Here, by integrating nanozyme activity and self-propelled movement that can promote the mass transfer and diffusion in solution, a novel dual-functional CDs-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 magnetic micromotor with high exposed active sites for the detection and removal of oxytetracycline (OTC) was prepared by a biotemplate method. The micromotor could move autonomously in aqueous solution containing H2O2, reaching a mean speed of 158.35 ± 11.64 μm/s in 5 wt% H2O2. Due to the synergy of motion and the CDs-enhanced peroxidase-like activity, the micromotor could easily detect OTC in aqueous solution with a low detection limit (0.291 μM), wide detection range (0–100 μM), high selectivity, and a high magnetic recovery rate (near 85 %). More importantly, these micromotors also enabled rapid degradation of OTC through Fenton and Fenton-like reactions, reaching 87.93 % in the presence of 5 wt% H2O2. This work could provide a new idea for detecting and removing antibiotics from water.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of MgAl-LDHs, MgAl-LDHs@MnO2, MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 and CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 (a), FT-IR spectra of CDs, MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 and CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 (b), N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (c) and pore size distribution (d) of Al2O3 microtube and CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4.
Full scanned XPS spectra (a), high-resolution spectra C 1 s (b), O 1 s (c), N 1 s (d), Mg 1 s (e), Al 2p (f), Mn 2p (g) and Fe 2p (h) of CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4.

Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors (a), FESEM images of silk template (b), MgAl-LDHs (c, d), MgAl-LDHs@MnO2 (e, f), CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 (g, h); TEM (i) and HRTEM (j) of CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4; EDS mapping images (k) of CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4.

Time-lapse images of the motion of CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors in 3 wt% H2O2 (a), schematic diagram of the motion mechanism (b) and the motion speed of the micromotor in different H2O2 concentrations (c), images of the motion of the micromotors under the control of an external magnetic field (d).

UV–visible absorption spectrum for the oxidation of three substrates catalyzed by CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 (a), UV–vis spectra of oxidized TMB of different samples in the absence of H2O2 (b), effect of scavengers on the absorbance of micromotors oxidized TMB in the absence of H2O2 (c), UV–vis spectra of different samples oxidizing TMB with H2O2 using BQ as •O2 – scavenger (d), UV–vis spectra of oxidized TMB of different samples in the presence of H2O2 (e), effect of scavengers on the absorbance of micromotors oxidized TMB in the presence of H2O2 (f), schematic representation of enzyme-like activities of micromotors (g).
The effect of time (a), TMB concentration (b), catalyst concentration (c), pH value (d), temperature (e) and H2O2 concentration (f) on the TMB + H2O2 + CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 reaction system.
Kinetics curve of TMB concentration and reaction rate (a), kinetics curve of H2O2 concentration and reaction rate (c) and corresponding Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal curve (b,d).

The mechanism of colorimetric detection of OTC based on CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors (a), UV–visible absorption spectra of micromotors catalyzed TMB at different OTC concentrations (b), the linear calibration plots for OTC detection (c), photograph of the colorimetric reaction system in the presence of different concentrations of OTC (d), photograph of the colorimetric reaction system in the selective experiment (e) and selective experiment of OTC colorimetric detection (f).

Degradation rate of different samples to OTC (a), degradation rate of OTC at different H2O2 concentrations (b) and different pH values (c), recovery experiment of OTC degradation by micromotors (d), XRD pattern of micromotors before and after OTC degradation (e), FESEM image of micromotors after OTC degradation (f).
EPR spectrums of DMPO-•OH (a) and DMPO-•O2 – (b) in the degraded system, proposed mechanism for OTC degradation.

Schematic illustration of synthesis and application of the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors, (I) synthesis of micromotors, (II) colotrimentric detection of OTC, (III) dynamic degradation of OTC by micromotors.
Comparison of the detection of OTC with other materials.
The recoveries of OTC standard solution in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dot LDHs@MnO2 tubular magnetic micromotors for detection and degradation of oxytetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibiotic residues in water are closely related to human health and environment. Therefore, it is of great significance to detect and remove antibiotics in water. Here, by integrating nanozyme activity and self-propelled movement that can promote the mass transfer and diffusion in solution, a novel dual-functional CDs-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 magnetic micromotor with high exposed active sites for the detection and removal of oxytetracycline (OTC) was prepared by a biotemplate method. The micromotor could move autonomously in aqueous solution containing H2O2, reaching a mean speed of 158.35 ± 11.64 μm/s in 5 wt% H2O2. Due to the synergy of motion and the CDs-enhanced peroxidase-like activity, the micromotor could easily detect OTC in aqueous solution with a low detection limit (0.291 μM), wide detection range (0–100 μM), high selectivity, and a high magnetic recovery rate (near 85 %). More importantly, these micromotors also enabled rapid degradation of OTC through Fenton and Fenton-like reactions, reaching 87.93 % in the presence of 5 wt% H2O2. This work could provide a new idea for detecting and removing antibiotics from water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The water pollution caused by the influx of drugs has aroused widespread concern. Tetracyclines (TCs) have good antibacterial properties and are heavily used in medical treatment [1]. Oxytetracycline (OTC) has become the second most used tetracycline due to its low cost, and this has also led to a large amount of contamination of OTC in the environment [2]. Many methods have been developed for OTC detection, such as electrochemical detection [3], high performance liquid chromatography [4], fluorescence detection [5], etc. However, these methods are limited by problems such as troublesome operation and long cycle time. Colorimetric detection assay has been used more frequently owing to its simplicity and efficiency [6].

In recent years, a variety of nanozymes materials with different enzyme-like properties have been prepared [7–10]. Nanozymes are more stable than natural enzymes in harsh environments, which allows them to have a wider range of applications [9,11]. The majority of these nanozymes are oxide-like [12,13], peroxide-like [14,15] and catalase-like [16,17] enzymes. The specific properties of these nanozymes can be utilized to construct a variety of colorimetric detection systems [18,19]. It has been demonstrated that target substances in aqueous solutions can be detected by a color change catalyzed by a nanozymes containing Fe3O4 or MnO2 [20–23]. These detection systems are not only selective for the target, but also have low detection limits. In order to prepare multifunctional assay materials with good biocompatibility, Liu et al. designed and prepared Fe3O4-Ag-MnO2 nanocomposites material [24], which possesses three properties of oxide-like, peroxide-like and catalase-like simultaneously. A multicolor colorimetric assay was constructed using this material with TMB for the detection of Staphylococcus aureus (detection limit was 3.7 cfu mL−1).

Nanozymes with peroxidase-like activity can directly reduce H2O2 to hydroxyl radicals (•OH) through the Fenton reaction [25,26]. Hydroxyl radical is the most important strong oxidizing species in advanced oxidation technologies, which can non-selectively degrade organic pollutants into small molecules, carbon dioxide, and water. Hormozi Jangi et al. built a bifunctional system using both MnO2 and SiO2@Fe3O4 nanozymes [27], and this system was able to accomplish the detection of H2O2 and the degradation of malachite green. Compared with a single component of the nanozyme, multinanozyme systems offer significant improvements in the detection and degradation of contaminants. Peng et al. synthesized α-MnO2 nanowires with good electrochemical properties by a simple hydrothermal method [28], which could be used as a sensor for rapid detection of H2O2 and L-ascorbic acid. Furthermore, such nanocrystals could oxidize phenol and chlorophenol in aqueous solutions of H2O2 and peroxymonosulfate. Furthermore, CDs-based hybrid with hierarchical structure, high specific surface area and exposed active sites can allow greater adsorption of H2O2, ultimately leading to more efficient decomposition of hydrogen peroxide into hydroxyl radicals •OH [29].

On the other hand, previous studies indicate that the effective contact between catalysts and reactants is a key factor in determining catalytic efficiency. Self-driven micro/nano-motors have been proven to have higher catalytic efficiency due to the increasing fixing effect caused by autonomous movement [30–33]. Most conventional micromotors utilize precious metals (Pt, Ag, etc.) for actuation, however, these metals are costly and harmful to the environment [34]. In recent years, MnO2, which is cheap, readily available and stable, has been used as a replacement for precious metals as the micromotors drive material. Muhammad Safdar et al. prepared six MnO2-based micromotors with different morphologies [34], in which the hollow sphere MnO2 particles can move up to 1600 μm/s. Ye et al. accomplished the large-scale synthesis of Fe-MnO2 core–shell micromotors using MnO2 instead of Pt [35]. These micromotors could effectively remove tetracycline antibiotics by Fenton reaction under neutral conditions. Our group reported a novel spherical Fe3O4@MnO2@HKUST-1 magnetic micromotor [36], which utilizes the MnO2 to drive and the magnetism of Fe3O4 to control the motion. In addition, this micromotor could realize the detection and degradation of hydroquinone.

Herein, based on the synergy of CDs-enhanced peroxidase-like activity and self-driven motion that can promote the mass transfer and diffusion in solution, we prepared a novel CDs-modified MgAl-LDHs/MnO2 magnetic micromotor for simultaneous detection and removal of antibiotic in water by a biotemplate route (Scheme 1 ). This magnetic micromotor could sensitively detect and quickly remove OTC in aqueous solution. In addition, their magnetism enables magnetic guidance of micromotor motion and magnetic recovery. This study provides a new idea for the preparation of micromotors with dual functions of detection and removal of antibiotics in water.

The silk was purchased from the local market. Na2CO3 (≥99.5 %), Al(NO3)3·9H2O (≥98 %), MgSO4·7H2O (≥99 %), ammonia solution (25 %), C6H5Na3O7·2H2O (≥99 %), poly(sodium 4-styrenesulfonate) (average Mw ∼ 70000, powder), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, ≥99 %), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 98 %), o-Phenylenediamine (OPD, AR), 2,2′-azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid ammonium salt) (ABTS, 98 %) and oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC, 95 %) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). C6H5O7(NH4)3 (98.5 %), CH3COOH (99.5 %), CH3COONa (99 %), FeCI3·6H2O (99 %), FeSO4·7H2O (≥99 %), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, AR), p-Benzoquinone (PBQ, 99 %) and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA, AR) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). MnSO4·H2O (≥99.0 %), KMnO4 (≥99.5 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, ≥30 %) and urea were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

The phase composition of the sample was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku SmartLab 9KW, JPN). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured with FT-IR spectrometer. Measurement of N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size by the BELSORP-max (MP) Brunauer-Emmet-Teller surface analyzer. Analysis of surface elements by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB Xi+, UK). The prepared samples were analyzed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, ZEISS Gemini300, GER) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100F, JPN) for microscopic morphology. Videos of the micro-motor movements were recorded by an optical microscope equipped with a digital camera (Tucsen EC300). The absorption spectra of the solutions were measured by UV–visible spectrophotometer (JINGHUA Instrument-754PC, CHN).

In a typical synthesis, the silk template were cleaned by sonication sequentially in distilled water containing 0.5 wt% Na2CO3 and kept at 100 ℃ for 30 min, and then dried at 80 ℃ for 24 h. The as-pretreated silk was put in a 200 mL of ethanol solution containing AlCl3 (20 wt%) under ultrasonic separation for 0.5 h, then dried at 70 ℃ for 24 h. Then the dried fibers were calcined at 800 ℃ for 2 h to obtain Al2O3 microtubes.

0.738 g MgSO4·7H2O, 0.375 g Al(NO3)3·9H2O and 0.36 g urea were dissolved in 30 mL distilled water, and the as-obtained Al2O3 microtubes were added to the mixed solution under continuous magnetic stirring for 30 min, and then transferred into autoclave in an electric oven at 160 ℃ for 10 h. The obtained MgAl-LDHs were washed several times with distilled water and then dried in air at 80 ℃.

100 mg of MgAl-LDHs were added to 60 mL of 1 mM MnSO4·H2O solution and stirred for 15 min. Then 60 mL of 1 mM KMnO4 solution was added into the mixture drop by drop under continuous magnetic stirring for 30 min. The product was then washed with deionized water to remove unreacted reactants and then dried in an oven at 60 ℃ for 4 h to obtain MgAl-LDHs@MnO2.

Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized using a chemical co-precipitation method [37]. 5 g FeCI3·6H2O and 9.673 g FeSO4·7H2O were added to 150 mL of deionized water, and then 10 mL of 28 % ammonia solution was slowly added to it under vigorous stirring for 30 min. The above solution was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 85 ℃ for 30 min. Then, 11.354 g of C6H5Na3O7·2H2O was added to the obtained mixture under stirring for 30 min. The obtained Fe3O4 nanoparticles were washed several times with distilled water and dried in an oven at 60 ℃ to obtain Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

20 mg of the obtained Fe3O4 were dispersed in 50 mL of 0.3 % aqueous poly(4-styrenesulfonate) solution. After ultrasonic dispersion for 20 min, the samples were washed three times with distilled water and then dispersed in 7 mL of methanol for 2 h. 100 mg of MgAl-LDHs@MnO2 were added to 50 mL of Fe3O4 suspension under stirring for 1 h to obtain MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors.

CDs were prepared according to reported method with minor modifications [38]. 2.5 g of C6H5O7(NH4)3 was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. Then the obtained solution was transferred into an autoclave in an oven and heated at 160 ℃ for 3 h. The suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and centrifuged at 5000 and 8000 rpm for 30 and 15 min, respectively. The obtained supernatant was stored at 4 ℃ for backup.

100 mg of MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 were added to 20 mL of diluted CDs suspension and stirred continuously for 1 h. The obtained CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors were washed with distilled water and dried in a vacuum oven at 50 ℃ for 12 h.

The peroxidase-like activity of samples was explored using TMB as substrate. 400 μL of 10 mM TMB solution, 400 μL of 100 mM H2O2 solution and 200 μL of 0.5 mg/mL micromotors suspension were added to 2 mL of acetate buffer (pH = 4) in sequence. The mixture was kept at room temperature for 10 min to reach reaction equilibrium. Then, the absorbance of the mixture at 652 nm was recorded using a UV spectrophotometer.

In addition, the reaction time (1–30 min), TMB concentration (0.1–10 mM), micromotor suspension concentration (0.05–2 mg/mL), pH (3–8), reaction temperature (25–60 ℃) and H2O2 concentration (10–150 mM) were varied sequentially to investigate the optimum conditions.
The steady-state kinetics of micromotors was explored by fixing the TMB and H2O2 concentrations separately under optimal conditions.

200 μL of 5 mM TMB solution, 200 μL of 100 mM H2O2 solution, and 200 μL of 0.5 mg/mL micromotor suspension were added sequentially to 2 mL of acetate buffer (pH = 4). Then 200 μL of different concentrations of OTC solutions (0–100 μM) were added to the mixture. The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 15 min and the absorbance at 652 nm was measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer after centrifugation treatment.
Selective detection of OTC relative to other species such as kanamycin, erythromycin, Cu2+, Ag+, Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2- and CO3 2– were investigated.

To test the sensing of OTC in real samples, different concentrations of OTC were added to tap water and lake water filtered through filter paper added to the system described above. Then, the absorbance at 652 nm was measured by UV spectrophotometer. Recovery values were calculated from the test results.

50 mg of micromotors were added to 50 mL of a 50 mg/L OTC solution and stored in a dark environment for 30 min to reach adsorption equilibrium. Subsequently, 5 mL of H2O2 (30 wt%) was added to the mixed solution. After each interval 5 mL of the upper layer solution was taken out. Then the absorbance of supernatant at 652 nm after centrifugation was measured and then the concentration of OTC was calculated based on the above colorimetric detection method.

The XRD patterns of the micromotor and each intermediate sample are shown in Fig. 1 a. The MgAl-LDHs nanosheets were highly crystalline with characteristic diffraction peaks at 11.63°, 23.59°, 34.97°, 39.54°, 47.21°, 61.03° and 62.19° corresponding to the (003), (006), (222), (225), (228), (600) and (603) planes of MgAl-LDHs (PDF#51–1525) [39]. The characteristic peaks of MnO2 (PDF#24–0735) could be observed at 28.58°, 37.18° and 72.32°, which were attributed to the (110), (101) and (310) planes of MnO2, respectively [40]. The relatively low intensity of diffraction peak indicated the poor crystallinity of MnO2 [41]. In addition, characteristic diffraction peaks at 19.98°, 30.19°, 35.69°, 43.28°, 53.97°, 57.28° and 62.82° corresponded to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) planes of Fe3O4 (PDF#75–0449) [42]. No clear diffraction peaks of carbon were observed in the XRD pattern of the final product, probably due to the low carbon content and relatively low diffraction intensity in the micromotors (Fig. S1) [43].

The micromotors were further characterized by FT-IR. The broad band at 3187 cm−1 (Fig. 1b) was caused by O–H stretching vibrations and interlayer water molecule stretching. Characteristic bands located at 1692 cm−1 and 1183 cm−1 from the CDs could be observed in the micromotors, corresponding to the telescopic vibrations of C = O and C-O–H, respectively [43]. The band at 776 cm−1 correspond to the [AlO6]3- group tensile vibration of MgAl-LDHs [44]. Bands at 622 cm−1 and 550 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of Fe-O (Fe3O4) [37] and Mn-O (MnO2) [36], respectively.

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the Al2O3 microtubes and CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors are shown in Fig. 1c. Both samples possessed type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, indicating the presence of mesopores in them. The formation mesoporous might be explained as follows: during the calcination of silk soaked with alumina precursor solution, the organic matter in silk template (sericin and fibroin [45]) was released in the form of gases, resulting in a large number of mesopores in microstructure. In addition, the MgAl-LDHs nanosheets grown vertically on the microtubule surface could also introduced a large number of mesopores for the micromotors. The corresponding pore size distribution in Fig. 1d also indicated the presence of mesopores in the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors. The specific surface area of the micromotor was 122.5 m2 g−1, which was much higher than that of Al2O3 microtubes (48.9 m2 g−1). The increase in surface area was mainly attributed to the formation of MgAl-LDHs and MnO2 nanosheets. This high specific surface area and porous structure provided more active sites, and this facilitated the treatment of pollutants.

The fabrication process of the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors is shown in Fig. 3 a. First, MgAl-LDH nanosheets were hydrothermally grown on Al2O3 microtubes prepared by the biotemplate method. Subsequently, MnO2 nanosheets were grown on MgAl-LDH nanosheets at room temperature. Finally, Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CDs were loaded on the surface of the micromotors.

The FESEM images of sike template, MgAl-LDHs, MgAl-LDHs@MnO2 and CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 are shown in Fig. 3b-Fig. 3h. Mulberry silk templates had a solid fiber morphology with a relatively smooth surface and an average diameter of 15 μm (Fig. 3b). The Al2O3 prepared from mulberry silk template retained the morphology of mulberry silk template to a certain extent, but the original solid fibers became hollow microtubular structures due to the oxidative removal of organic substances from the mulberry silk template during the calcination process. Vertically standing MgAl-LDHs nanosheets were grown on the outer surface of Al2O3 microtubules after hydrothermal treatment (Fig. 3c and Fig. 3d). Al2O3 acted as a seed layer to provide Al for the growth of MgAl-LDHs, the bonding strength of MgAl-LDHs is sufficient high to ensure that it will not peel from the micromotor during the movement of micromotor. These nanosheets function as skeleton for the subsequent growth of MnO2 nanosheets. As shown in Fig. 3e and Fig. 3f, the size of the MnO2 nanosheets was much smaller than that of the MgAl-LDHs nanosheets, and each MgAl-LDHs nanosheet was wrapped in multiple MnO2 nanosheets, forming a unique 3D structure composed of two different sizes of nanosheets. Meanwhile, MnO2 nanosheets were also observed on the inner walls of microtubules. Furthermore, as can be seen from Fig. 3g and Fig. 3h, many nanoparticles can be observed in the space formed by the interweaving of nanosheets. Combined with the above XRD results, it can be inferred that these are Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The uniform distribution of Fe3O4 nanoparticles indicates the successful magnetization of micromotors. Fig. S2 shows the TEM and HRTEM images of CDs, indicating the successful synthesis of CDs. The diameter of the CDs was ∼ 7 nm. HRTEM images depicted a lattice spacing of ∼ 0.3215 nm, which closely matched the CQDs (002) lattice plane. CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors were characterized using TEM (Fig. 3i) and HRTEM (Fig. 3j). The lattice spacing of 0.3025 nm was attributed to the (110) plane of MnO2 and the lattice spacing of 0.4861 nm is the (111) side of Fe3O4. CDs with lattice spacing of 0.3202 (002) could also be observed distributed on MnO2 and Fe3O4. These results were in agreement with XRD. The EDS mapping images (Fig. 3k) confirmed the presence of C, O, Mg, Al, Mn and Fe in the micromotor, and these elements were distributed in the micromotors.

The movement of CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotor in 1 wt%, 3 wt% and 5 wt% H2O2 (containing 0.5 wt% sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS)) are shown in Video S1. Fig. 4 a shows the time-lapse images of the micromotors in 3 wt% H2O2. It could be noticed that O2 bubbles ejected from one end of the micromotor tube body and drove the micromotors in a forward spiral motion. These oxygen bubbles were generated by the decomposition of H2O2 catalyzed by MnO2 (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c shows the effect of H2O2 concentration on the motion speed of micromotors. The speed of micromotor increased with the increase of H2O2 concentration. The velocities of the micromotor in H2O2 solutions with concentrations of 1 wt%, 3 wt% and 5 wt% were 41.95 ± 3.49 μm/s, 82.00 ± 7.32 μm/s and 158.35 ± 11.64 μm/s, respectively.

Video S2 shows the motion behavior of the micromotors with different lengths moving in 3 wt% H2O2 solution. The relationship between speed and length is shown in Fig. S3 (number of samples is 5). The motion velocity decreased slightly with the increasing length of micromotor. This is the result of the combined action of two factors. On the one hand, longer micromotors loaded more MnO2, which catalyze H2O2 to produce more bubbles to propel the micromotors, thereby increasing their speed. On the other hand, the increased length would lead to an increase in the weight of micromotor, thereby reducing their speed. The results indicate that the effect of weight on the speed of micromotor was dominate. Video S3 shows the movement of MgAl-LDHs@MnO2 micromotor, MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotor and CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotor in 3 wt% H2O2 solution. Fig. S4 shows their corresponding speeds. MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotor had a decrease in motion speed relative to MgAl-LDHs@MnO2 micromotor, which was due to the increased weight of the micromotor after loading it with Fe3O4. There is a slight increase in the speed of motion of the micromotor loaded with CDs, which could be attributed to the rapid adsorption of H2O2 by the CDs thereby accelerating the decomposition of H2O2 [29]. In addition, we compared the motions of the micromotors in different pH environments (Video S4 and Fig. S5). The micromotors could move at every pH environment. As the pH decreases, the speed of the micromotors decreases gradually. It was due to the fact that MnO2 decomposed part of the H2O2 into active species under acidic conditions, which resulted a decrease in the O2 that drove the micromotors motion [27].

In addition, the motion of the micromotors in an external magnetic field is shown in Video S5, and the corresponding time-lapse images are shown in Fig. 4d. The motion direction of the micromotors could be precisely controlled by an external magnetic field. When the magnetic field was removed, the micromotor resumes its original motion, indicating the excellent magnetic guidance property of micromotors.

Initially, TMB (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine), OPD (o-Phenylenediamine) and ABTS (2,2′-azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid ammonium salt)) were chosen as substrates to verify the selectivity of the CDs-MgAl-LDH@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors for substrate. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the micromotor could catalyze three substrates (TMB, OPD and ABTS) to create different colors (blue, yellow and green) with the maximum absorption wavelengths at 652 nm, 450 nm and 417 nm, respectively. Because the absorption intensity of TMB was the largest among three substrates, TMB was finally chosen as the chromogenic substrate for subsequent experiments.

To confirm the oxidase-like characteristic of the micromotors, the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors and a range of intermediate products were introduced into the buffer TMB. The absorbances of the solutions from each system were then measured. As shown in Fig. 5b, the absorbances of the systems containing samples TMB + MgAl-LDHs@MnO2 and TMB + MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 exhibited stronger absorbance at 652 nm compared to TMB alone, suggesting that MnO2 and Fe3O4 in the micromotors possess certain oxidase-like properties. Furthermore, it was observed that the introduction of CDs did not significantly enhance the oxidase-like properties of the micromotors. In order to explore the reactive oxygen species (ROS) acting in the system, capture experiments were performed using isopropyl alcohol (IPA), p-Benzoquinone (PBQ) and ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) as the scavengers of hydroxyl radical (•OH), superoxide anion (•O2 –) and vacancies (h+). As shown in Fig. 5c, only the addition of PBQ leads to a significant decrease in the absorbance of the TMB + CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 system, indicating that the enzymatic-like properties of the micromotors depend largely on •O2 –.

To study the peroxidase-like properties of the micromotors, the oxidase-like properties of the micromotors were shielded using PBQ. Subsequently, H2O2 introduced into the mixed solutions. As shown in Fig. 5d, TMB + H2O2 + PBQ + MgAl-LDHs@MnO2, TMB + H2O2 + PBQ + MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 and TMB + H2O2 + PBQ + CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 exhibited stronger absorbance at 625 nm compared to TMB + H2O2 + PBQ. This indicated that in the micromotors, in addition to MnO2 and Fe3O4 having a strong peroxidase-like properties, CDs also possessed such activities. In addition, a comparison of the absorbance at 652 nm between the two systems, TMB + CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 and TMB + H2O2 + PBQ + CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4, revealed that the peroxidase-like activities of the micromotors was higher than the oxidase-like activities. CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors possessed dual enzyme-like activities and exhibited stronger catalytic ability towards substrates (Fig. 5e).

ROS capture experiments were performed to investigate the types of free radicals produced by the micro motors in H2O2. The addition of both IPA and PBQ resulted in significant decreases in the absorbances (Fig. 5f), indicating that •OH and •O2 – were the major reactive species in the catalytic reactions. The possible mechanisms for the generations •OH and •O2 – could be explained as follows (Fig. 5g): in the acidic conditions, the Fe3+/Fe2+ and Mn4+/Mn3+ redox pairs in the micromotors could decompose H2O2 to •OH (Eq. 1 and Eq. 2) [31]. Due to the small size and large specific surface area of CDs, it could easily adsorb H2O2 [29]. Furthermore, during the catalytic TMB oxidation process, the enhanced electron transfer between the amine groups of TMB and CDs increased the surface electron density, which accelerated the decomposition of H2O2 and thus produced more •OH [29]. In addition, in acidic conditions MnO2 could react with H2O2 to produce O2 and further converted to •O2 – (Eq. 3 and Eq. 4) [31]. Eventually, •OH and •O2 – were able to oxidize TMB to oxTMB (Eq. 5).

Fig. 6 a-Fig. 6f show the effect of reaction time, TMB concentration, catalyst concentration, pH, reaction temperature and H2O2 concentration on the peroxidase-like properties of CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotor. The absorbance of the reaction solution at 652 nm was close to equilibrium at a reaction time of 15 min (Fig. 6a). This meant that the TMB oxidized by the micromotors reached equilibrium after 15 min of reaction in the system. The color of the system was bright blue and the absorbance reached moderate values when the concentrations of TMB and the dose of micromotor added to the reaction solution were 5 mM and 0.5 mg/mL, respectively (Fig. 6b and Fig. 6c). The pH of the solution had a strong effect on the oxidation of TMB, and the enzyme-like activity of the micromotors was strongest when the pH of the system was 4 (Fig. 6d). In addition, the absorbance of the reaction solution was decreased at 652 nm with the increase in temperature (Fig. 6e), therefore the optimum temperature for the reaction was 25 ℃. The optimum concentration of H2O2 in the mixed solution for the reaction was 50 mM (Fig. 6f). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

To further investigate the peroxidase-like properties of the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors, the steady-state kinetics were determined by varying the concentrations of TMB and H2O2 solutions while keeping other conditions constant. The Michaelis-Menten curves of TMB and H2O2 were measured experimentally and shown in Fig. 7 a and Fig. 7c. This indicated that the catalytic effect of the micromotors on the substrate was similar to that of the natural enzyme. The Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal curve (eq. (6) [49] could be used to calculate the Michaelis constant (Km ) and the maximum reaction velocity (Vmax ) of the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors for TMB and H2O2.
(6)

where ν represented the initial reaction rate and S was the substrate concentration. Km was able to directly respond to the affinity of the catalyst for the substrate. The final calculated Km of the micromotors for TMB and H2O2 were 0.488 mM and 1.625 mM (Fig. 7b and Fig. 7d). The Km of the micromotors for TMB was close to that of natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP, 0.434 mM), which indicated that it had a similar affinity to HRP for TMB. And the Km of micromotors to H2O2 was lower than that of HRP (3.7 mM), which meant it had a better affinity for H2O2 than HRP. This result was highly related to the 3D morphology of the micromotors, which provides a larger specific surface area and more active sites.

Based on the excellent peroxidase-like properties of the micromotor, we proposed a colorimetric strategy for OTC detection. As shown in Fig. 8 b-Fig. 8d, the color of the system gradually changed from light blue to dark blue with the increase of OTC content in the system. The absorbance (A) of the system at 652 nm and the OTC concentration (C) showed a good linear relationship in the range of 0–100 μM. The linear regression equation was A = 0.0103C + 0.3561, and the squared correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9975. The detection limit (LOD) of the colorimetric system could be calculated using the following equation [50].
(7)
LOD = 3 × S D / m
where SD represents the standard deviation and m is the slope of the regression equation. The LOD of the colorimetric system was calculated to be 0.291 μM. A comparison of the present system with other reported assays is summarized in Table 1 . The detection performance of the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors were better than most of the reported methods.

The detection mechanism was shown in Fig. 8a. OTC surface contains a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, which could promote its complexation with Fe3O4 of the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors [57]. The complexation between them could enhance the peroxidase-like activity of the micromotors, which could promote the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2.

To study the selectivity of the micromotors for OTC detection, we introduced some interfering substances (kanamycin, erythromycin, Cu2+, Ag+, Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2- and CO3 2–) to the colorimetric system. As shown in Fig. 8e and Fig. 8f, the change in absorbance of the system containing OTC was the largest relative to the original system. This indicates that the present colorimetric system possessed excellent resistance to interference.

In addition, tap water and lake water were chosen for the experimental subjects to verify the practicality of this method. Different concentrations of OTC were added to the actual water samples and their concentrations were detected by colorimetric system. As shown in Table 2 , the spiked recoveries of the samples ranged from 93.65 % to 105.30 % and the relative standard deviations ranged from 2.06 % to 8.22 %. These results indicate that the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors could be used to detect the OTC in the actual water samples.

Since the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors could react with H2O2 to generate a large number of free radicals (•OH and •O2 –), we investigated the degradation ability of micromotors for OTC. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the degradation rate of OTC was only 6.89 % in the presence of H2O2 alone, indicating that it was difficult to degrade OTC by H2O2 only. The removal rate of OTC by MgAl-LDHs was 24.92 %, which was mainly attributed to its excellent adsorption capacity. In addition, MgAl-LDHs@MnO2 and MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 showed 48.48 % and 76.82 % degradation capacities for OTC under the same conditions. This was mainly because both Fe3O4 and MnO2 could undergo Fenton and Fenton-like reactions in an acidic environment, producing large amounts •OH to degrade OTC. CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 + H2O2 system exhibits the highest degradation among all samples (82.05 %), indicating that the introduction of CDs played a facilitating role in the degradation of OTC. This is mainly attributed to the fact that CDs could enhance electron transfer thereby accelerating the degradation of OTC [59].

As shown in Fig. 9b, the degradation efficiency of the micromotors for OTC gradually increased with the increasing concentration of H2O2 in the system. The removal rate of OTC by the micromotors was 87.93 % in the presence of 5 wt% H2O2. This is due to more free radicals generated from the decomposition of H2O2 by the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors. In addition, we also investigated the effect of pH on the degradation system, and the results were shown in Fig. 9c. The degradation efficiency of the system was highest at pH = 3. This was related to the optimal working conditions for the Fenton species in the micromotors [60]. Table S1 shows the degradation effect of the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors on OTC compared to other reported materials. The removal of OTC by the micromotors was stronger than part of the reported materials.

Based on the magnetic properties of the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors, the micromotors could be recycled with a magnet after degradation is completed, as shown in Fig. S6. The recovery of micromotors by an external magnetic field is shown in Table S2, with an average recovery rate of nearly 85 %. The recovered micromotors were reused for degradation and the degradation rate was maintained at 77.05 % after three cycles (Fig. 9d). The XRD patterns of the micromotors before and after use are shown in Fig. 9e, and the main diffraction peaks were basically unchanged before and after use. FESEM image of the micromotors after degradation of OTC (Fig. 9f) shows little change in the surface topography of the micromotors. The 3D structure on the surface of the micromotors possessed excellent stability, and it would not be detached during the motion. These results indicated that the micromotors possessed high chemical stability and reusability during the degradation of OTC.

According to previous explorations, the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors in the degradation system were able to decompose H2O2 to produce •OH and •O2 –. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) demonstrated the presence of •OH and •O2 – in the system (Fig. 10 a and Fig. 10b). Four signal peaks for DMPO-•OH and six typical signal peaks for DMPO-•O2 – could be observed. This was consistent with the reported signal peaks for DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 – [61,62]. The possible degradation mechanism of OTC was shown in Fig. 10c. Fe3O4 and MnO2 of the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors could produce •OH by Fenton and Fenton-like reactions. The Fe3+/Fe2+ and Mn4+/Mn3+ could catalyze the formation of •OH from H2O2 in acidic environment (Eq. 1 and Eq. 2). The O2 in the system attached to the surface of the micromotors and converted to •O2 – (Eq. 3 and Eq. 4) [31]. In addition, the small size of CDs could easily adsorb H2O2 and enhance electron transfer thus accelerating the production of reactive oxygen species [29,59]. Eventually, •OH and •O2 – of the system completed degradation of the OTC (Eq. (8).
(8)
∙
O H / ∙
O 2 -
+ O T C → d e g r a d a t i o n p r o d u c t s

To further analyze the possible degradation pathways of OTC by the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors, LC/MS measurement was used to detect possible degradation intermediates. The TOF-MS spectrum was shown in Fig. S7, and the structural and chemical formulae of the possible degradation intermediates were summarized in Table S3. The possible degradation pathways were proposed based on the results of TOF-MS and exhibited in Fig. S8. The degradation pathway was consistent with previous report [63]. OTC (m/z = 461) was converted to P1 (m/z = 475) by quinone and hydroxylation reactions. P1 decomposed to P3 (m/z = 338) via decarboxamidation, deamidation, dihydroxylation and demethylation pathways. Then, P5 (m/z = 302) was formed by ring opening and converted to P7 (m/z = 226) by decarboxylation and dehydroxylation. In addition, OTC could be converted to P2 (m/z = 362) by removing N-methyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups and utilizing the deamidation reactions, followed by the elimination of the groups (carbonyl, methyl and hydroxyl) to transform to P4 (m/z = 318) [64]. P4 with hydroxyl and amino groups removed was fragmented to P6 (m/z = 274) and continued to be converted to P8 (m/z = 242) [63,65]. Afterwards these products continued to be oxidized to small molecule products and mineralized to H2O and CO2 [63]. Fig. S9 shows the change in the total organic carbon (TOC) concentration in the degradation system. The result showed that the removal of TOC was about 68.37 % in 180 min. These results demonstrated the good degradation ability of the CDs-MgAl-LDHs@MnO2/Fe3O4 micromotors in removing OTC.

In summary, we prepared a novel CDs-modified MgAl-LDHs@MnO2 magnetic micromotor by a biological template method for the detection and removal of OTC. A unique 3D structure consisting of MgAl-LDHs nanosheets and MnO2 nanosheets was formed on the surface of the micromotor. The autonomous motion of the micromotor was realized by the decomposition of H2O2, and the maximum velocity was 158.35 ± 11.64 μm/s in the presence of 5 wt% H2O2. Based on the CDs-enhanced peroxidase-like activity, this micromotors not only could sensitively detect OTC in the linear concentration range of 0–100 μM (LOD of 0.291 μM), but also could rapidly remove OTC with a maximum removal efficiency of 87.93 %. This 3D multilayer structured micromotors with dual function would have promising applications in environmental treatment.
